steps.title: Fastest check: compare a table or graph
steps.content: For an equivalent-expression question, enter the original expression and the answer choices in a calculator table or graph. The correct choice must match for several inputs, not just one. The matching choice is C, $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left({x}^{6}+2\right)$.

steps.title: Algebra check
steps.content: Use the work below to confirm the identity for every allowed input. This matters because one random matching input is only a quick screen, not a proof that two expressions are always equivalent.

steps.title: Factor the expression
steps.content: Factor $6x^8y^2 + 12x^2y^2$ by pulling out the greatest common factor. Both terms share $6x^2y^2$: $6x^8y^2 + 12x^2y^2 = 6x^2y^2(x^6 + 2)$.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left(2{x}^{6}\right)$: Expanding gives $12x^8y^2$, which is missing the $12x^2y^2$ term entirely. ✗
  • Choice B — $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left({x}^{4}\right)$: Expanding gives $6x^6y^2$, which matches neither term of the original expression. ✗
  • Choice C — $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left({x}^{6}+2\right)$: Expanding gives $6x^8y^2 + 12x^2y^2$ — exactly the original expression. ✓
  • Choice D — $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left({x}^{4}+2\right)$: Expanding gives $6x^6y^2 + 12x^2y^2$, but the original has $x^8$, not $x^6$. ✗
That matches Choice C — $6{x}^{2}{y}^{2} \left({x}^{6}+2\right)$.
